data.title: Megapuzzle
data.solution:
import re

sentence = input()
match = re.search(r"(?:[^A-Za-z]|^)(?:[Mm][Yy]|[Yy]?[Oo][Uu][Rr])[^A-Za-z]+([A-Za-z]+)", sentence)
assert match is not None

print("Mega" + match[1][0].lower() + match[1][1:])
data.statement:
Sometimes you want to feel like a superhero, and one way to achieve this is to make the ordinary extraordinary, by prefixing ordinary names with "Mega"!

You are provided with a sentence which contains a noun prefixed by a possessive adjective among {{my}}, {{your}} and {{our}} (case-insensitive). You must find the noun, only make the first character lowercase (keep the original casing for the rest of the word), and add {{Mega}} in front; then, and must print the obtained word.

For example, {{Paris}} becomes {{Megaparis}}, {{pants}} become {{Megapants}}, {{PascalCase}} becomes {{MegapascalCase}} and {{camelCase}} becomes {{MegacamelCase}}.

In this context, words are sets of uppercase and lowercase letters from A to Z. There will always be exactly one possessive adjective from the list provided above in the input sentence.

testCases.title: My lowercase megaword
testCases.testIn: Oh no, I forgot my pants at home!

testCases.title: My lowercase megaword
testCases.testIn: Did you know my dog who was very cute?

testCases.testIn: The army is actually using your plans.

testCases.testIn: We ought to pour it, you know, our wine.

testCases.title: Our lowercase megaword
testCases.testIn: The man of the hour is our hero of yesterday!

testCases.testIn: Our State is the best out there.

testCases.title: Your uppercase megaword
testCases.testIn: Your GLFM might actually be better than the Github equivalent.

testCases.title: Our mixed case megaword
testCases.testIn: If you made a meme that worked, it is now OuR MeMe.

testCases.title: Our mixed case megaword
testCases.testIn: You know what, oUr lETTERs seem to have wEiRd casing.

testCases.title: My mixed case megaword
testCases.testIn: mY wIfE iS mAd i sEnD hEr mEsSaGeS lIkE tHiS lOl

testCases.title: My mixed case megaword
testCases.testIn: It is mY hOnOR to welcome you to this town.

testCases.title: Mysterious!
testCases.testIn: Mystery novels are my jam.

testCases.title: Mysterious!
testCases.testIn: It's mythical how they managed to win over our hearts and minds!

testCases.title: Test it yourself!
testCases.testIn: It's yours if you want my money, it's not like I'm going to stop you.

testCases.title: Test it yourself!
testCases.testIn: You did it yourself? But it's not your thing though!

testCases.title: Ouranographists hate him
testCases.testIn: Ourselves, we are not particularly fan of your music specifically.

testCases.title: Ouranographists hate him
testCases.testIn: An ourobouros can be seen in our sky, if you look deep enough.

testCases.title: CAP
testCases.testIn: IT IS TIME TO TAKE YOUR STUFF AND GO HOME!!

testCases.title: CAP
testCases.testIn: MY TAILOR IS RICH, OK?

lastVersion.statementHTML:
Goal

Sometimes you want to feel like a superhero, and one way to achieve this is to make the ordinary extraordinary, by prefixing ordinary names with "Mega"!

You are provided with a sentence which contains a noun prefixed by a possessive adjective among my, your and our (case-insensitive). You must find the noun, only make the first character lowercase (keep the original casing for the rest of the word), and add Mega in front; then, and must print the obtained word.

For example, Paris becomes Megaparis, pants become Megapants, PascalCase becomes MegapascalCase and camelCase becomes MegacamelCase.

In this context, words are sets of uppercase and lowercase letters from A to Z. There will always be exactly one possessive adjective from the list provided above in the input sentence.
Input
First line: sentence to process.
Output
First line: Obtained megaword.
Constraints
0 < length(sentence) < 255
sentence is ASCII-encoded.
Example
Input
Oh no, I forgot my pants at home!
Output
Megapants
